translation: I have told you these things so that you may have peace in me. In the world you have trouble; but take courage, I have conquered the world.

alternate_translations.translation: I have said these things to you so that you may have peace—because of me. In the world you will have tribulation; but be of good cheer, I have overcome the world.
alternate_translations.notes: “θλῖψιν ἔχετε” can be rendered as “tribulation” and “ἐν τῷ κόσμῳ” as “in the world,” while “θαρασεῖτε” is often translated idiomatically as “be of good cheer.” “ἐγὼ νενίκηκα” strongly conveys a completed victory (“have overcome/conquered”).

alternate_translations.translation: I told you these things, so that you might have peace in me. You will have affliction in the world; however, take heart—I have won the world.
alternate_translations.notes: “θλῖψις” is frequently translated “affliction.” “θαρασεῖτε” can also be “take heart.” “ἔχω/νενίκηκα” indicates a past, abiding result: “I have won.”

notes: This passage is from the New Testament (Johannine context). “Εἰρήνη” here is not merely the absence of conflict but a peace grounded “in/through” Christ (“ἐν ἐμοί”). “θλῖψιν” denotes pressure, distress, or trouble (often “tribulation”). The final clause emphasizes Christ’s already-accomplished victory over “the world” (κόσμος), meaning the hostile system/world-order opposed to God.
